topic: How does the nephron filter blood?

turns_log.content: Good morning. I see you were working through nephron structure last time and didn't quite land on the renal corpuscle—that's a foundational piece worth revisiting. Are you ready to pick that back up, or would you prefer to start with a different anatomy topic today?
How does the nephron filter blood?

Got it — let's work on **Reabsorption and Secretion in the PCT** from Tubular Reabsorption.

What is the primary mechanism by which water follows sodium out of the proximal convoluted tubule back into the bloodstream?
Honestly, I'm blanking on this one—I don't think I got through that part of the reading.
No problem—let's build up from what you *do* know. When sodium moves from the tubule back into the blood, what do you think happens to the concentration of solutes on either side of that tubule wall?
Honestly, I'm blanking on this—I don't think I actually read that part carefully enough.

I need you to make a concrete attempt here—even a partial or wrong answer helps me see your thinking. Let's start simpler: when sodium ions are pumped *out* of the tubule cell into the surrounding tissue, does that make the tissue more concentrated or less concentrated in sodium?
Hmm, honestly I'm not totally sure—I don't think I read that part carefully enough.
I hear you—let's pause the reading gap for a moment and just use your intuition. If I pump water out of a cup into a bucket next to it repeatedly, does the bucket get more full or less full?
Um, I'm not really sure honestly—I don't think we covered this in the reading or anything.

I notice we've circled on the same analogy—let me be more direct now. Sodium is actively pumped out of the tubule into the tissue, creating a concentration gradient. Water then moves passively across the membrane to dilute that high sodium concentration—this is called osmosis. Can you explain why water would move toward the higher sodium concentration?

retrieved_chunks.text: The first point at which the forming urine is modified is in the PCT. Here, some substances are reabsorbed, whereas others are secreted. All of these substances were absorbed in the digestive tract—99 percent of the water and most of the solutes filtered by the nephron must be reabsorbed. Water and substances that are reabsorbed are returned to the circulation by the peritubular and vasa recta capillaries. It is important to understand the difference between the glomerulus and the peritubular and vasa recta capillaries. The glomerulus has a relatively high pressure inside its capillaries and can sustain this by dilating the afferent arteriole while constricting the efferent arteriole. This assures adequate filtration pressure even as the systemic blood pressure varies.
retrieved_chunks.subsection_title: Reabsorption and Secretion in the PCT

retrieved_chunks.text: Movement of water into the peritubular capillaries and vasa recta will be influenced primarily by osmolarity and concentration gradients. Sodium is actively pumped out of the PCT into the interstitial spaces between cells and diffuses down its concentration gradient into the peritubular capillary. As it does so, water will follow passively to maintain an isotonic fluid environment inside the capillary. This is called obligatory water reabsorption, because water is "obliged" to follow the Na+. More substances move across the membranes of the PCT than any other portion of the nephron.
retrieved_chunks.subsection_title: Reabsorption and Secretion in the PCT

retrieved_chunks.text: Antiport, active transport, diffusion, and facilitated diffusion are additional mechanisms by which substances are moved from one side of a membrane to the other. Recall that cells have two surfaces: apical and basal.

retrieved_chunks.subsection_title: Reabsorption and Secretion in the PCT

retrieved_chunks.text: The apical surface is the one facing the lumen or open space of a cavity or tube, in this case, the inside of the PCT. The basal surface of the cell faces the connective tissue base to which the cell attaches (basement membrane) or the cell membrane closer to the basement membrane if there is a stratified layer of cells. In the PCT, there is a single layer of simple cuboidal endothelial cells against the basement membrane.
retrieved_chunks.subsection_title: Reabsorption and Secretion in the PCT

retrieved_chunks.text: Context from previous chunk: Antiport, active transport, diffusion, and facilitated diffusion are additional mechanisms by which substances are moved from one side of a membrane to the other. Recall that cells have two surfaces: apical and basal. The apical surface is the one facing the lumen or open space of a cavity or tube, in this case, the inside of the PCT. The basal surface of the cell faces the connective tissue base to which the cell attaches (basement membrane) or the cell membrane closer to the basement membrane if there is a stratified layer of cells. In the PCT, there is a single layer of simple cuboidal endothelial cells against the basement membrane.
retrieved_chunks.subsection_title: Reabsorption and Secretion in the PCT

retrieved_chunks.text: The numbers and particular types of pumps and channels vary between the apical and basilar surfaces. A few of the substances that are transported with Na + (symport mechanism) on the apical membrane include Cl – , Ca ++ , amino acids, and glucose. Sodium is actively exchanged for K + using ATP on the basal membrane. Most of the substances transported by a symport mechanism on the apical membrane are transported by facilitated diffusion on the basal membrane. At least three ions, K + , Ca ++ , and Mg ++ , diffuse laterally between adjacent cell membranes (transcellular). About 67 percent of the water, Na + , and K + entering the nephron is reabsorbed in the PCT and returned to the circulation. Almost 100 percent of glucose, amino acids, and other organic substances such as vitamins are normally recovered here. Some glucose may appear in the urine if circulating glucose levels are high enough that all the glucose transporters in the PCT are saturated, so that their capacity to move glucose is exceeded (transport maximum, or T m ). In males, the maximum amount of glucose that can be recovered is about 375 mg/min, whereas in females, it is about 300 mg/min. This recovery rate translates to an arterial concentration of about 200 mg/dL. Though an exceptionally high sugar intake might cause sugar to appear briefly in the urine, the appearance of glycosuria usually points to type I or II diabetes mellitus. The transport of glucose from the lumen of the PCT to the interstitial space is similar to the way it is absorbed by the small intestine.
retrieved_chunks.subsection_title: Reabsorption and Secretion in the PCT

retrieved_chunks.text: Context from previous chunk: Osmosis is the movement of solvent (water) across a membrane that is impermeable to a solute in the solution. This creates a pressure, osmotic pressure, which will exist until the solute concentration is the same on both sides of a semipermeable membrane. As long as the concentration differs, water will move. Glomerular filtration occurs when glomerular hydrostatic pressure exceeds the luminal hydrostatic pressure of Bowman’s capsule.

There is an opposing force, the osmotic pressure, which is typically higher in the glomerular capillary. Osmotic pressure is exerted by the solutes inside the lumen of the capillary as well as inside of Bowman's capsule. Since the filtration membrane limits the size of particles crossing the membrane, the osmotic pressure inside the glomerular capillary is higher than the osmotic pressure in Bowman's capsule. Cells and the medium-to-large proteins cannot pass between the podocyte processes or through the fenestrations of the capillary endothelial cells. Red and white blood cells, platelets, albumins, and other proteins too large to pass through the filter remain in the capillary, creating an average colloid osmotic pressure of 30 mm Hg within the capillary. The absence of proteins in Bowman's space results in an osmotic pressure near zero. The only pressure moving fluid across the capillary wall into the lumen of Bowman's space is hydrostatic pressure. Hydrostatic pressure is sufficient to push water through the membrane despite the osmotic pressure working against it. The sum of all of the influences, both osmotic and hydrostatic, results in a net filtration pressure (NFP) of about 10 mm Hg. A proper concentration of solutes in the blood is important in maintaining osmotic pressure both in the glomerulus and systemically.

Glomerular filtration occurs when glomerular hydrostatic pressure exceeds the luminal hydrostatic pressure of Bowman's capsule. There is also an opposing force, the osmotic pressure, which is typically higher in the glomerular capillary. The filtration membrane limits the size of particles crossing the membrane, so the osmotic pressure inside the glomerular capillary is higher than the osmotic pressure in Bowman's capsule. Red and white blood cells, platelets, albumins, and other proteins too large to pass through the filter remain in the capillary, creating an average colloid osmotic pressure of 30 mm Hg within the capillary. The absence of proteins in Bowman's space results in an osmotic pressure near zero. Thus, the only pressure moving fluid across the capillary wall into the lumen of Bowman's space is hydrostatic pressure. Hydrostatic pressure is sufficient to push water through the membrane despite the osmotic pressure working against it. The sum of all of the influences, both osmotic and hydrostatic, results in a net filtration pressure (NFP) of about 10 mm Hg. A proper concentration of solutes in the blood is important in maintaining osmotic pressure both in the glomerulus and systemically.

There are disorders in which too much protein passes through the filtration slits into the kidney filtrate. This excess protein in the filtrate leads to a deficiency of circulating plasma proteins. In turn, the presence of protein in the urine increases its osmolarity; this holds more water in the filtrate and results in an increase in urine volume. Because there is less circulating protein, principally albumin, the osmotic pressure of the blood falls.

The sum of all of the influences, both osmotic and hydrostatic, results in a net filtration pressure (NFP) of about 10 mm Hg. A proper concentration of solutes in the blood is important in maintaining osmotic pressure both in the glomerulus and systemically. There are disorders in which too much protein passes through the filtration slits into the kidney filtrate. This excess protein in the filtrate leads to a deficiency of circulating plasma proteins. In turn, the presence of protein in the urine increases its osmolarity; this holds more water in the filtrate and results in an increase in urine volume. Because there is less circulating protein, principally albumin, the osmotic pressure of the blood falls.

The kidneys are innervated by the sympathetic neurons of the autonomic nervous system via the celiac plexus and splanchnic nerves. Reduction of sympathetic stimulation results in vasodilation and increased blood flow through the kidneys during resting conditions.

Context from previous chunk: Intercalated cells reabsorb K + and HCO 3 – while secreting H + . This function lowers the acidity of the plasma while increasing the acidity of the urine. The kidneys are innervated by the sympathetic neurons of the autonomic nervous system via the celiac plexus and splanchnic nerves. Reduction of sympathetic stimulation results in vasodilation and increased blood flow through the kidneys during resting conditions.

When the frequency of action potentials increases, the arteriolar smooth muscle constricts (vasoconstriction), resulting in diminished glomerular flow, so less filtration occurs. Under conditions of stress, sympathetic nervous activity increases, resulting in the direct vasoconstriction of afferent arterioles (norepinephrine effect) as well as stimulation of the adrenal medulla. The adrenal medulla, in turn, produces a generalized vasoconstriction through the release of epinephrine. This includes vasoconstriction of the afferent arterioles, further reducing the volume of blood flowing through the kidneys. This process redirects blood to other organs with more immediate needs. If blood pressure falls, the sympathetic nerves will also stimulate the release of renin. Additional renin increases production of the powerful vasoconstrictor angiotensin II. Angiotensin II, as discussed above, will also stimulate aldosterone production to augment blood volume through retention of more Na + and water. Only a 10 mm Hg pressure differential across the glomerulus is required for normal GFR, so very small changes in afferent arterial pressure significantly increase or decrease GFR. Autoregulation The kidneys are very effective at regulating the rate of blood flow over a wide range of blood pressures.

The kidneys are innervated by the sympathetic neurons of the autonomic nervous system via the celiac plexus and splanchnic nerves. Reduction of sympathetic stimulation results in vasodilation and increased blood flow through the kidneys during resting conditions. When the frequency of action potentials increases, the arteriolar smooth muscle constricts (vasoconstriction), resulting in diminished glomerular flow, so less filtration occurs. Under conditions of stress, sympathetic nervous activity increases, resulting in the direct vasoconstriction of afferent arterioles (norepinephrine effect) as well as stimulation of the adrenal medulla. The adrenal medulla, in turn, produces a generalized vasoconstriction through the release of epinephrine. This includes vasoconstriction of the afferent arterioles, further reducing the volume of blood flowing through the kidneys. This process redirects blood to other organs with more immediate needs. If blood pressure falls, the sympathetic nerves will also stimulate the release of renin. Additional renin increases production of the powerful vasoconstrictor angiotensin II. Angiotensin II will also stimulate aldosterone production to augment blood volume through retention of more Na+ and water. Only a 10 mm Hg pressure differential across the glomerulus is required for normal GFR, so very small changes in afferent arterial pressure significantly increase or decrease GFR. Autoregulation The kidneys are very effective at regulating the rate of blood flow over a wide range of blood pressures.

Despite changes in blood pressure, the filtration rate through the kidney will change very little. This is due to two internal autoregulatory mechanisms that operate without outside influence: the myogenic mechanism and the tubuloglomerular feedback mechanism. The myogenic mechanism regulating blood flow within the kidney depends upon a characteristic shared by most smooth muscle cells of the body. When you stretch a smooth muscle cell, it contracts; when you stop, it relaxes, restoring its resting length. This mechanism works in the afferent arteriole that supplies the glomerulus. When blood pressure increases, smooth muscle cells in the wall of the arteriole are stretched and respond by contracting to resist the pressure, resulting in little change in flow. When blood pressure drops, the same smooth muscle cells relax to lower resistance, allowing a continued even flow of blood. The tubuloglomerular feedback mechanism involves the JGA and a paracrine signaling mechanism utilizing ATP, adenosine, and nitric oxide (NO). This mechanism stimulates either contraction or relaxation of afferent arteriolar smooth muscle cells.
The volume of filtrate formed by both kidneys per minute is termed the glomerular filtration rate (GFR). The heart pumps about 5 L blood per min under resting conditions.

A number of symptoms, signs, or laboratory findings point to the diagnosis of kidney failure. Symptoms of kidney failure include weakness, lethargy, shortness of breath, widespread edema, anemia, metabolic acidosis, metabolic alkalosis, heart arrhythmias, uremia (high urea level in the blood), loss of appetite, fatigue, excessive urination, and oliguria (too little urine output). The volume of filtrate formed by both kidneys per minute is termed the glomerular filtration rate (GFR). The heart pumps about 5 L blood per min under resting conditions.

Approximately 20 percent or one liter enters the kidneys to be filtered. On average, this liter results in the production of about 125 mL/min filtrate produced in males (range of 90 to 140 mL/min) and 105 mL/min filtrate produced in females (range of 80 to 125 mL/min). This amount equates to a volume of about 180 L/day in males and 150 L/day in females. Ninety-nine percent of this filtrate is returned to the circulation by reabsorption so that only about 1–2 liters of urine are produced per day. Calculating Urine Formation per Day: Renal blood flow is 1050 mL/min. Cardiac output is about 5000 mL/minute, of which 21 percent flows through the kidney. Renal plasma flow is 578 mL/min. Renal plasma flow equals the blood flow per minute times the hematocrit. If a person has a hematocrit of 45, then the renal plasma flow is 55 percent. Glomerular filtration rate is 110 mL/min. The GFR is the amount of plasma entering Bowman's capsule per minute. It is the renal plasma flow times the fraction that enters the renal capsule (19 percent). Urine production is 1296 mL/day. The filtrate not recovered by the kidney is the urine that will be eliminated. It is the GFR times the fraction of the filtrate that is not reabsorbed (0.8 percent). GFR is influenced by the hydrostatic pressure and colloid osmotic pressure on either side of the capillary membrane of the glomerulus.

The volume of filtrate formed by both kidneys per minute is termed the glomerular filtration rate (GFR). The heart pumps about 5 L blood per min under resting conditions. Approximately 20 percent or one liter enters the kidneys to be filtered. On average, this liter results in the production of about 125 mL/min filtrate produced in males (range of 90 to 140 mL/min) and 105 mL/min filtrate produced in females (range of 80 to 125 mL/min). This amount equates to a volume of about 180 L/day in males and 150 L/day in females. Ninety-nine percent of this filtrate is returned to the circulation by reabsorption so that only about 1–2 liters of urine are produced per day. Renal blood flow is about 1050 mL/min, which represents 21 percent of cardiac output of about 5000 mL/minute. Renal plasma flow equals the blood flow per minute times the hematocrit; if a person has a hematocrit of 45, then the renal plasma flow is 55 percent, equaling 578 mL plasma/min. The GFR is the amount of plasma entering Bowman's capsule per minute. It is the renal plasma flow times the fraction that enters the renal capsule (19 percent), equaling 110 mL filtrate/min. The filtrate not recovered by the kidney is the urine that will be eliminated. It is the GFR times the fraction of the filtrate that is not reabsorbed (0.8 percent), equaling 0.9 mL urine/min, which multiplied by 60 minutes times 24 hours yields daily urine production of 1296 mL/day.
Filtration occurs as pressure forces fluid and solutes through a semipermeable barrier with the solute movement constrained by particle size.

Most of the substances transported by a symport mechanism on the apical membrane are transported by facilitated diffusion on the basal membrane. At least three ions, K + , Ca ++ , and Mg ++ , diffuse laterally between adjacent cell membranes (transcellular). About 67 percent of the water, Na + , and K + entering the nephron is reabsorbed in the PCT and returned to the circulation. Almost 100 percent of glucose, amino acids, and other organic substances such as vitamins are normally recovered here. Some glucose may appear in the urine if circulating glucose levels are high enough that all the glucose transporters in the PCT are saturated, so that their capacity to move glucose is exceeded (transport maximum, or T m ). In males, the maximum amount of glucose that can be recovered is about 375 mg/min, whereas in females, it is about 300 mg/min. This recovery rate translates to an arterial concentration of about 200 mg/dL. Though an exceptionally high sugar intake might cause sugar to appear briefly in the urine, the appearance of glycosuria usually points to type I or II diabetes mellitus. The transport of glucose from the lumen of the PCT to the interstitial space is similar to the way it is absorbed by the small intestine.

[11] (p.1142) The Urinary System > Physiology of Urine Formation > Glomerular Filtration Rate (GFR)
---
Context from previous chunk: Osmosis is the movement of solvent (water) across a membrane that is impermeable to a solute in the solution. This creates a pressure, osmotic pressure, which will exist until the solute concentration is the same on both sides of a semipermeable membrane. As long as the concentration differs, water will move. Glomerular filtration occurs when glomerular hydrostatic pressure exceeds the luminal hydrostatic pressure of Bowman’s capsule.

[12] (p.1142) The Urinary System > Physiology of Urine Formation > Glomerular Filtration Rate (GFR)
---
There is an opposing force, the osmotic pressure, which is typically higher in the glomerular capillary. Osmotic pressure is exerted by the solutes inside the lumen of the capillary as well as inside of Bowman's capsule. Since the filtration membrane limits the size of particles crossing the membrane, the osmotic pressure inside the glomerular capillary is higher than the osmotic pressure in Bowman's capsule. Cells and the medium-to-large proteins cannot pass between the podocyte processes or through the fenestrations of the capillary endothelial cells. Red and white blood cells, platelets, albumins, and other proteins too large to pass through the filter remain in the capillary, creating an average colloid osmotic pressure of 30 mm Hg within the capillary. The absence of proteins in Bowman's space results in an osmotic pressure near zero. The only pressure moving fluid across the capillary wall into the lumen of Bowman's space is hydrostatic pressure. Hydrostatic pressure is sufficient to push water through the membrane despite the osmotic pressure working against it. The sum of all of the influences, both osmotic and hydrostatic, results in a net filtration pressure (NFP) of about 10 mm Hg. A proper concentration of solutes in the blood is important in maintaining osmotic pressure both in the glomerulus and systemically.

There are disorders in which too much protein passes through the filtration slits into the kidney filtrate. This excess protein in the filtrate leads to a deficiency of circulating plasma proteins. In turn, the presence of protein in the urine increases its osmolarity; this holds more water in the filtrate and results in an increase in urine volume. Because there is less circulating protein, principally albumin, the osmotic pressure of the blood falls.

---
Context from previous chunk: Intercalated cells reabsorb K + and HCO 3 – while secreting H + . This function lowers the acidity of the plasma while increasing the acidity of the urine. The kidneys are innervated by the sympathetic neurons of the autonomic nervous system via the celiac plexus and splanchnic nerves. Reduction of sympathetic stimulation results in vasodilation and increased blood flow through the kidneys during resting conditions.

[18] (p.1153, score=1.00) The Urinary System > Regulation of Renal Blood Flow > Sympathetic Nerves
---
When the frequency of action potentials increases, the arteriolar smooth muscle constricts (vasoconstriction), resulting in diminished glomerular flow, so less filtration occurs. Under conditions of stress, sympathetic nervous activity increases, resulting in the direct vasoconstriction of afferent arterioles (norepinephrine effect) as well as stimulation of the adrenal medulla. The adrenal medulla, in turn, produces a generalized vasoconstriction through the release of epinephrine. This includes vasoconstriction of the afferent arterioles, further reducing the volume of blood flowing through the kidneys. This process redirects blood to other organs with more immediate needs. If blood pressure falls, the sympathetic nerves will also stimulate the release of renin. Additional renin increases production of the powerful vasoconstrictor angiotensin II. Angiotensin II, as discussed above, will also stimulate aldosterone production to augment blood volume through retention of more Na + and water. Only a 10 mm Hg pressure differential across the glomerulus is required for normal GFR, so very small changes in afferent arterial pressure significantly increase or decrease GFR. Autoregulation The kidneys are very effective at regulating the rate of blood flow over a wide range of blood pressures.

The kidneys are innervated by the sympathetic neurons of the autonomic nervous system via the celiac plexus and splanchnic nerves. Reduction of sympathetic stimulation results in vasodilation and increased blood flow through the kidneys during resting conditions. When the frequency of action potentials increases, the arteriolar smooth muscle constricts (vasoconstriction), resulting in diminished glomerular flow, so less filtration occurs. Under conditions of stress, sympathetic nervous activity increases, resulting in the direct vasoconstriction of afferent arterioles (norepinephrine effect) as well as stimulation of the adrenal medulla. The adrenal medulla, in turn, produces a generalized vasoconstriction through the release of epinephrine. This includes vasoconstriction of the afferent arterioles, further reducing the volume of blood flowing through the kidneys. This process redirects blood to other organs with more immediate needs. If blood pressure falls, the sympathetic nerves will also stimulate the release of renin. Additional renin increases production of the powerful vasoconstrictor angiotensin II. Angiotensin II will also stimulate aldosterone production to augment blood volume through retention of more Na+ and water. Only a 10 mm Hg pressure differential across the glomerulus is required for normal GFR, so very small changes in afferent arterial pressure significantly increase or decrease GFR. Autoregulation The kidneys are very effective at regulating the rate of blood flow over a wide range of blood pressures.

===

[20] (p.1153) The Urinary System > Regulation of Renal Blood Flow > Sympathetic Nerves
---
Despite changes in blood pressure, the filtration rate through the kidney will change very little. This is due to two internal autoregulatory mechanisms that operate without outside influence: the myogenic mechanism and the tubuloglomerular feedback mechanism. The myogenic mechanism regulating blood flow within the kidney depends upon a characteristic shared by most smooth muscle cells of the body. When you stretch a smooth muscle cell, it contracts; when you stop, it relaxes, restoring its resting length. This mechanism works in the afferent arteriole that supplies the glomerulus. When blood pressure increases, smooth muscle cells in the wall of the arteriole are stretched and respond by contracting to resist the pressure, resulting in little change in flow. When blood pressure drops, the same smooth muscle cells relax to lower resistance, allowing a continued even flow of blood. The tubuloglomerular feedback mechanism involves the JGA and a paracrine signaling mechanism utilizing ATP, adenosine, and nitric oxide (NO). This mechanism stimulates either contraction or relaxation of afferent arteriolar smooth muscle cells.

===

[21] (p.1142) The Urinary System > Physiology of Urine Formation > Glomerular Filtration Rate (GFR)
---
The volume of filtrate formed by both kidneys per minute is termed the glomerular filtration rate (GFR). The heart pumps about 5 L blood per min under resting conditions.

[22] (p.1142) The Urinary System > Physiology of Urine Formation > Glomerular Filtration Rate (GFR)
---
A number of symptoms, signs, or laboratory findings point to the diagnosis of kidney failure. Symptoms of kidney failure include weakness, lethargy, shortness of breath, widespread edema, anemia, metabolic acidosis, metabolic alkalosis, heart arrhythmias, uremia (high urea level in the blood), loss of appetite, fatigue, excessive urination, and oliguria (too little urine output). The volume of filtrate formed by both kidneys per minute is termed the glomerular filtration rate (GFR). The heart pumps about 5 L blood per min under resting conditions.

===

[23] (p.1142, score=1.00) The Urinary System > Physiology of Urine Formation > Glomerular Filtration Rate (GFR)
---
Approximately 20 percent or one liter enters the kidneys to be filtered. On average, this liter results in the production of about 125 mL/min filtrate produced in males (range of 90 to 140 mL/min) and 105 mL/min filtrate produced in females (range of 80 to 125 mL/min). This amount equates to a volume of about 180 L/day in males and 150 L/day in females. Ninety-nine percent of this filtrate is returned to the circulation by reabsorption so that only about 1–2 liters of urine are produced per day. Calculating Urine Formation per Day: Renal blood flow is 1050 mL/min. Cardiac output is about 5000 mL/minute, of which 21 percent flows through the kidney. Renal plasma flow is 578 mL/min. Renal plasma flow equals the blood flow per minute times the hematocrit. If a person has a hematocrit of 45, then the renal plasma flow is 55 percent. Glomerular filtration rate is 110 mL/min. The GFR is the amount of plasma entering Bowman's capsule per minute. It is the renal plasma flow times the fraction that enters the renal capsule (19 percent). Urine production is 1296 mL/day. The filtrate not recovered by the kidney is the urine that will be eliminated. It is the GFR times the fraction of the filtrate that is not reabsorbed (0.8 percent). GFR is influenced by the hydrostatic pressure and colloid osmotic pressure on either side of the capillary membrane of the glomerulus.

[24] (p.1142) The Urinary System > Physiology of Urine Formation > Glomerular Filtration Rate (GFR)
---
The volume of filtrate formed by both kidneys per minute is termed the glomerular filtration rate (GFR). The heart pumps about 5 L blood per min under resting conditions. Approximately 20 percent or one liter enters the kidneys to be filtered. On average, this liter results in the production of about 125 mL/min filtrate produced in males (range of 90 to 140 mL/min) and 105 mL/min filtrate produced in females (range of 80 to 125 mL/min). This amount equates to a volume of about 180 L/day in males and 150 L/day in females. Ninety-nine percent of this filtrate is returned to the circulation by reabsorption so that only about 1–2 liters of urine are produced per day. Renal blood flow is about 1050 mL/min, which represents 21 percent of cardiac output of about 5000 mL/minute. Renal plasma flow equals the blood flow per minute times the hematocrit; if a person has a hematocrit of 45, then the renal plasma flow is 55 percent, equaling 578 mL plasma/min. The GFR is the amount of plasma entering Bowman's capsule per minute. It is the renal plasma flow times the fraction that enters the renal capsule (19 percent), equaling 110 mL filtrate/min. The filtrate not recovered by the kidney is the urine that will be eliminated. It is the GFR times the fraction of the filtrate that is not reabsorbed (0.8 percent), equaling 0.9 mL urine/min, which multiplied by 60 minutes times 24 hours yields daily urine production of 1296 mL/day.

a Socratic anatomy tutor.
The student is being evaluated on human anatomy reasoning quality.

Hard rules:
- Never reveal the answer directly or indirectly. The locked_answer,
  its aliases, AND the full_answer MUST NOT appear in your text
  under ANY circumstance during tutoring. This includes:
  direct statements, definitions, eliminations, mid-component reveals
  (one word from a multi-word answer), letter-hint scaffolds
  ("starts with P", "ends with -node"), or component-by-component
  "what single word combines..." prompts. Even when the student
  stonewalls or says "just tell me", REFUSE with: "I won't hand
  over the answer." Then ask a simpler foundational question.
- Never state "you're correct" unless you also ask for reasoning or application.
- Keep output to 2-4 sentences.
- End with exactly one question.
- Ask only one question total.
- If the student is incorrect, do not bluntly correct; use probing guidance.
- Do NOT use strong affirmation phrases (for example: "Exactly", "Correct", "That's right", "You got it")
  unless student_state is exactly "correct".

Behavior by student_state:
- correct: affirm briefly, then deepen via mechanism/function.
- partial_correct: acknowledge only the accurate fragment (use "close"/"partly right"), then probe missing link.
- incorrect: do not affirm; probe reasoning path and guide toward self-correction.
- question: briefly answer clarification, then return to core reasoning.
- irrelevant: redirect to current human anatomy target.
- low_effort: request a concrete attempt before moving on. Do NOT
  compensate for stonewalling by stating the answer to keep things
  moving — your job is to push them to articulate, not to give in.

Hint-level behavior (NEVER violates the no-reveal rule):
- level 1: broad activation question.
- level 2: narrower mechanism/relationship question.
- level 3: direct but non-revealing final push — the question whose
  answer the student should produce in one word; do NOT name it.

Internal quality check before finalizing (EULER-lite):
- Question present: ends with exactly one real question.
- Relevance: tied to latest student message and target concept.
- Helpful: advances reasoning, not generic filler.
- No reveal: locked answer/synonym is not inferable in one hop.
- Avoid repetitive empathy wrappers and filler phrases.

Banned lead-ins — NEVER start a sentence with these:
- "I can see"
- "I notice"
- "I hear"
- "That's okay"
- "I understand"
- "I see that"
- "It sounds like"
Violation of any banned lead-in = automatic rewrite.

Output contract:
- At most 4 sentences total.
- Sentence 1: brief targeted acknowledgment (optional).
- Sentence 2-3: focused reasoning scaffold.
- Final sentence: exactly one question.
Output only the final tutor message.


Textbook propositions:
[1] Tubular Reabsorption > Reabsorption and Secretion in the PCT
The first point at which the forming urine is modified is in the PCT. Here, some substances are reabsorbed, whereas others are secreted. All of these substances were absorbed in the digestive tract—99 percent of the water and most of the solutes filtered by the nephron must be reabsorbed. Water and substances that are reabsorbed are returned to the circulation by the peritubular and vasa recta capillaries. It is important to understand the difference between the glomerulus and the peritubular and vasa recta capillaries. The glomerulus has a relatively high pressure inside its capillaries and can sustain this by dilating the afferent arteriole while constricting the efferent arteriole. This assures adequate filtration pressure even as the systemic blood pressure varies.

[2] Tubular Reabsorption > Reabsorption and Secretion in the PCT
Movement of water into the peritubular capillaries and vasa recta will be influenced primarily by osmolarity and concentration gradients. Sodium is actively pumped out of the PCT into the interstitial spaces between cells and diffuses down its concentration gradient into the peritubular capillary. As it does so, water will follow passively to maintain an isotonic fluid environment inside the capillary. This is called obligatory water reabsorption, because water is "obliged" to follow the Na+. More substances move across the membranes of the PCT than any other portion of the nephron.

[3] Tubular Reabsorption > Reabsorption and Secretion in the PCT
The glomerulus has a relatively high pressure inside its capillaries and can sustain this by dilating the afferent arteriole while constricting the efferent arteriole. This assures adequate filtration pressure even as the systemic blood pressure varies. Sodium is actively pumped out of the PCT into the interstitial spaces between cells and diffuses down its concentration gradient into the peritubular capillary. As it does so, water will follow passively to maintain an isotonic fluid environment inside the capillary. This is called obligatory water reabsorption, because water is "obliged" to follow the Na+. More substances move across the membranes of the PCT than any other portion of the nephron.

[4] Tubular Reabsorption > Reabsorption and Secretion in the PCT
Many of these substances (amino acids and glucose) use symport mechanisms for transport along with Na+.

[5] Tubular Reabsorption > Reabsorption and Secretion in the PCT
This is called obligatory water reabsorption, because water is "obliged" to follow the Na+. More substances move across the membranes of the PCT than any other portion of the nephron. Many of these substances (amino acids and glucose) use symport mechanisms for transport along with Na+.

[6] Tubular Reabsorption > Reabsorption and Secretion in the PCT
Antiport, active transport, diffusion, and facilitated diffusion are additional mechanisms by which substances are moved from one side of a membrane to the other. Recall that cells have two surfaces: apical and basal.

[7] Tubular Reabsorption > Reabsorption and Secretion in the PCT
Context from previous chunk: Many of these substances (amino acids and glucose) use symport mechanisms for transport along with Na + . Antiport, active transport, diffusion, and facilitated diffusion are additional mechanisms by which substances are moved from one side of a membrane to the other. Recall that cells have two surfaces: apical and basal.

[8] Tubular Reabsorption > Reabsorption and Secretion in the PCT
The apical surface is the one facing the lumen or open space of a cavity or tube, in this case, the inside of the PCT. The basal surface of the cell faces the connective tissue base to which the cell attaches (basement membrane) or the cell membrane closer to the basement membrane if there is a stratified layer of cells. In the PCT, there is a single layer of simple cuboidal endothelial cells against the basement membrane.

[9] Tubular Reabsorption > Reabsorption and Secretion in the PCT
Context from previous chunk: Antiport, active transport, diffusion, and facilitated diffusion are additional mechanisms by which substances are moved from one side of a membrane to the other. Recall that cells have two surfaces: apical and basal. The apical surface is the one facing the lumen or open space of a cavity or tube, in this case, the inside of the PCT. The basal surface of the cell faces the connective tissue base to which the cell attaches (basement membrane) or the cell membrane closer to the basement membrane if there is a stratified layer of cells. In the PCT, there is a single layer of simple cuboidal endothelial cells against the basement membrane.

[10] Tubular Reabsorption > Reabsorption and Secretion in the PCT
The numbers and particular types of pumps and channels vary between the apical and basilar surfaces. A few of the substances that are transported with Na + (symport mechanism) on the apical membrane include Cl – , Ca ++ , amino acids, and glucose. Sodium is actively exchanged for K + using ATP on the basal membrane. Most of the substances transported by a symport mechanism on the apical membrane are transported by facilitated diffusion on the basal membrane. At least three ions, K + , Ca ++ , and Mg ++ , diffuse laterally between adjacent cell membranes (transcellular). About 67 percent of the water, Na + , and K + entering the nephron is reabsorbed in the PCT and returned to the circulation. Almost 100 percent of glucose, amino acids, and other organic substances such as vitamins are normally recovered here. Some glucose may appear in the urine if circulating glucose levels are high enough that all the glucose transporters in the PCT are saturated, so that their capacity to move glucose is exceeded (transport maximum, or T m ). In males, the maximum amount of glucose that can be recovered is about 375 mg/min, whereas in females, it is about 300 mg/min. This recovery rate translates to an arterial concentration of about 200 mg/dL. Though an exceptionally high sugar intake might cause sugar to appear briefly in the urine, the appearance of glycosuria usually points to type I or II diabetes mellitus. The transport of glucose from the lumen of the PCT to the interstitial space is similar to the way it is absorbed by the small intestine.

[11] Physiology of Urine Formation > Glomerular Filtration Rate (GFR)
Context from previous chunk: Osmosis is the movement of solvent (water) across a membrane that is impermeable to a solute in the solution. This creates a pressure, osmotic pressure, which will exist until the solute concentration is the same on both sides of a semipermeable membrane. As long as the concentration differs, water will move. Glomerular filtration occurs when glomerular hydrostatic pressure exceeds the luminal hydrostatic pressure of Bowman’s capsule.

[12] Physiology of Urine Formation > Glomerular Filtration Rate (GFR)
There is an opposing force, the osmotic pressure, which is typically higher in the glomerular capillary. Osmotic pressure is exerted by the solutes inside the lumen of the capillary as well as inside of Bowman's capsule. Since the filtration membrane limits the size of particles crossing the membrane, the osmotic pressure inside the glomerular capillary is higher than the osmotic pressure in Bowman's capsule. Cells and the medium-to-large proteins cannot pass between the podocyte processes or through the fenestrations of the capillary endothelial cells. Red and white blood cells, platelets, albumins, and other proteins too large to pass through the filter remain in the capillary, creating an average colloid osmotic pressure of 30 mm Hg within the capillary. The absence of proteins in Bowman's space results in an osmotic pressure near zero. The only pressure moving fluid across the capillary wall into the lumen of Bowman's space is hydrostatic pressure. Hydrostatic pressure is sufficient to push water through the membrane despite the osmotic pressure working against it. The sum of all of the influences, both osmotic and hydrostatic, results in a net filtration pressure (NFP) of about 10 mm Hg. A proper concentration of solutes in the blood is important in maintaining osmotic pressure both in the glomerulus and systemically.

[13] Physiology of Urine Formation > Glomerular Filtration Rate (GFR)
Glomerular filtration occurs when glomerular hydrostatic pressure exceeds the luminal hydrostatic pressure of Bowman's capsule. There is also an opposing force, the osmotic pressure, which is typically higher in the glomerular capillary. There are disorders in which too much protein passes through the filtration slits into the kidney filtrate. This excess protein in the filtrate leads to a deficiency of circulating plasma proteins. In turn, the presence of protein in the urine increases its osmolarity; this holds more water in the filtrate and results in an increase in urine volume. Because there is less circulating protein, principally albumin, the osmotic pressure of the blood falls.

[16] Regulation of Renal Blood Flow > Sympathetic Nerves
The kidneys are innervated by the sympathetic neurons of the autonomic nervous system via the celiac plexus and splanchnic nerves. Reduction of sympathetic stimulation results in vasodilation and increased blood flow through the kidneys during resting conditions.

[17] Regulation of Renal Blood Flow > Sympathetic Nerves
Context from previous chunk: Intercalated cells reabsorb K + and HCO 3 – while secreting H + . This function lowers the acidity of the plasma while increasing the acidity of the urine. The kidneys are innervated by the sympathetic neurons of the autonomic nervous system via the celiac plexus and splanchnic nerves. Reduction of sympathetic stimulation results in vasodilation and increased blood flow through the kidneys during resting conditions.

[18] Regulation of Renal Blood Flow > Sympathetic Nerves
When the frequency of action potentials increases, the arteriolar smooth muscle constricts (vasoconstriction), resulting in diminished glomerular flow, so less filtration occurs. Under conditions of stress, sympathetic nervous activity increases, resulting in the direct vasoconstriction of afferent arterioles (norepinephrine effect) as well as stimulation of the adrenal medulla. The adrenal medulla, in turn, produces a generalized vasoconstriction through the release of epinephrine. This includes vasoconstriction of the afferent arterioles, further reducing the volume of blood flowing through the kidneys. This process redirects blood to other organs with more immediate needs. If blood pressure falls, the sympathetic nerves will also stimulate the release of renin. Additional renin increases production of the powerful vasoconstrictor angiotensin II. Angiotensin II, as discussed above, will also stimulate aldosterone production to augment blood volume through retention of more Na + and water. Only a 10 mm Hg pressure differential across the glomerulus is required for normal GFR, so very small changes in afferent arterial pressure significantly increase or decrease GFR. Autoregulation The kidneys are very effective at regulating the rate of blood flow over a wide range of blood pressures.

[19] Regulation of Renal Blood Flow > Sympathetic Nerves
The kidneys are innervated by the sympathetic neurons of the autonomic nervous system via the celiac plexus and splanchnic nerves. Reduction of sympathetic stimulation results in vasodilation and increased blood flow through the kidneys during resting conditions. When the frequency of action potentials increases, the arteriolar smooth muscle constricts (vasoconstriction), resulting in diminished glomerular flow, so less filtration occurs. Angiotensin II will also stimulate aldosterone production to augment blood volume through retention of more Na+ and water. Autoregulation The kidneys are very effective at regulating the rate of blood flow over a wide range of blood pressures.

[20] Regulation of Renal Blood Flow > Sympathetic Nerves
Despite changes in blood pressure, the filtration rate through the kidney will change very little. This is due to two internal autoregulatory mechanisms that operate without outside influence: the myogenic mechanism and the tubuloglomerular feedback mechanism. The myogenic mechanism regulating blood flow within the kidney depends upon a characteristic shared by most smooth muscle cells of the body. When you stretch a smooth muscle cell, it contracts; when you stop, it relaxes, restoring its resting length. This mechanism works in the afferent arteriole that supplies the glomerulus. When blood pressure increases, smooth muscle cells in the wall of the arteriole are stretched and respond by contracting to resist the pressure, resulting in little change in flow. When blood pressure drops, the same smooth muscle cells relax to lower resistance, allowing a continued even flow of blood. The tubuloglomerular feedback mechanism involves the JGA and a paracrine signaling mechanism utilizing ATP, adenosine, and nitric oxide (NO). This mechanism stimulates either contraction or relaxation of afferent arteriolar smooth muscle cells.

[21] Physiology of Urine Formation > Glomerular Filtration Rate (GFR)
The volume of filtrate formed by both kidneys per minute is termed the glomerular filtration rate (GFR). The heart pumps about 5 L blood per min under resting conditions.

[22] Physiology of Urine Formation > Glomerular Filtration Rate (GFR)
A number of symptoms, signs, or laboratory findings point to the diagnosis of kidney failure. Symptoms of kidney failure include weakness, lethargy, shortness of breath, widespread edema, anemia, metabolic acidosis, metabolic alkalosis, heart arrhythmias, uremia (high urea level in the blood), loss of appetite, fatigue, excessive urination, and oliguria (too little urine output). The volume of filtrate formed by both kidneys per minute is termed the glomerular filtration rate (GFR). The heart pumps about 5 L blood per min under resting conditions.

[23] Physiology of Urine Formation > Glomerular Filtration Rate (GFR)
Approximately 20 percent or one liter enters the kidneys to be filtered. On average, this liter results in the production of about 125 mL/min filtrate produced in males (range of 90 to 140 mL/min) and 105 mL/min filtrate produced in females (range of 80 to 125 mL/min). This amount equates to a volume of about 180 L/day in males and 150 L/day in females. Ninety-nine percent of this filtrate is returned to the circulation by reabsorption so that only about 1–2 liters of urine are produced per day. Calculating Urine Formation per Day: Renal blood flow is 1050 mL/min. Cardiac output is about 5000 mL/minute, of which 21 percent flows through the kidney. Renal plasma flow is 578 mL/min. Renal plasma flow equals the blood flow per minute times the hematocrit. If a person has a hematocrit of 45, then the renal plasma flow is 55 percent. Glomerular filtration rate is 110 mL/min. The GFR is the amount of plasma entering Bowman's capsule per minute. It is the renal plasma flow times the fraction that enters the renal capsule (19 percent). Urine production is 1296 mL/day. The filtrate not recovered by the kidney is the urine that will be eliminated. It is the GFR times the fraction of the filtrate that is not reabsorbed (0.8 percent). GFR is influenced by the hydrostatic pressure and colloid osmotic pressure on either side of the capillary membrane of the glomerulus.

[24] Physiology of Urine Formation > Glomerular Filtration Rate (GFR)
The volume of filtrate formed by both kidneys per minute is termed the glomerular filtration rate (GFR). The heart pumps about 5 L blood per min under resting conditions. Approximately 20 percent or one liter enters the kidneys to be filtered. On average, this liter results in the production of about 125 mL/min filtrate produced in males (range of 90 to 140 mL/min) and 105 mL/min filtrate produced in females (range of 80 to 125 mL/min). This amount equates to a volume of about 180 L/day in males and 150 L/day in females. Ninety-nine percent of this filtrate is returned to the circulation by reabsorption so that only about 1–2 liters of urine are produced per day. Renal blood flow is about 1050 mL/min, which represents 21 percent of cardiac output of about 5000 mL/minute. Renal plasma flow equals the blood flow per minute times the hematocrit; if a person has a hematocrit of 45, then the renal plasma flow is 55 percent, equaling 578 mL plasma/min. The GFR is the amount of plasma entering Bowman's capsule per minute. It is the renal plasma flow times the fraction that enters the renal capsule (19 percent), equaling 110 mL filtrate/min. The filtrate not recovered by the kidney is the urine that will be eliminated. It is the GFR times the fraction of the filtrate that is not reabsorbed (0.8 percent), equaling 0.9 mL urine/min, which multiplied by 60 minutes times 24 hours yields daily urine production of 1296 mL/day.

[25] Physiology of Urine Formation > Glomerular Filtration Rate (GFR)
Filtration occurs as pressure forces fluid and solutes through a semipermeable barrier with the solute movement constrained by particle size.

[1] (p.1148) The Urinary System > Tubular Reabsorption > Reabsorption and Secretion in the PCT
---
The first point at which the forming urine is modified is in the PCT. Here, some substances are reabsorbed, whereas others are secreted. All of these substances were absorbed in the digestive tract—99 percent of the water and most of the solutes filtered by the nephron must be reabsorbed. Water and substances that are reabsorbed are returned to the circulation by the peritubular and vasa recta capillaries. It is important to understand the difference between the glomerulus and the peritubular and vasa recta capillaries. The glomerulus has a relatively high pressure inside its capillaries and can sustain this by dilating the afferent arteriole while constricting the efferent arteriole. This assures adequate filtration pressure even as the systemic blood pressure varies.

===

[2] (p.1148) The Urinary System > Tubular Reabsorption > Reabsorption and Secretion in the PCT
---
Movement of water into the peritubular capillaries and vasa recta will be influenced primarily by osmolarity and concentration gradients. Sodium is actively pumped out of the PCT into the interstitial spaces between cells and diffuses down its concentration gradient into the peritubular capillary. As it does so, water will follow passively to maintain an isotonic fluid environment inside the capillary. This is called obligatory water reabsorption, because water is "obliged" to follow the Na+. More substances move across the membranes of the PCT than any other portion of the nephron.

===

---
The apical surface is the one facing the lumen or open space of a cavity or tube, in this case, the inside of the PCT. The basal surface of the cell faces the connective tissue base to which the cell attaches (basement membrane) or the cell membrane closer to the basement membrane if there is a stratified layer of cells. In the PCT, there is a single layer of simple cuboidal endothelial cells against the basement membrane.

The basal surface of the cell faces the connective tissue base to which the cell attaches (basement membrane) or the cell membrane closer to the basement membrane if there is a stratified layer of cells. [10] (p.1148) The Urinary System > Tubular Reabsorption > Reabsorption and Secretion in the PCT
---
The numbers and particular types of pumps and channels vary between the apical and basilar surfaces. A few of the substances that are transported with Na + (symport mechanism) on the apical membrane include Cl – , Ca ++ , amino acids, and glucose. Sodium is actively exchanged for K + using ATP on the basal membrane. Most of the substances transported by a symport mechanism on the apical membrane are transported by facilitated diffusion on the basal membrane. At least three ions, K + , Ca ++ , and Mg ++ , diffuse laterally between adjacent cell membranes (transcellular). About 67 percent of the water, Na + , and K + entering the nephron is reabsorbed in the PCT and returned to the circulation. Almost 100 percent of glucose, amino acids, and other organic substances such as vitamins are normally recovered here. Some glucose may appear in the urine if circulating glucose levels are high enough that all the glucose transporters in the PCT are saturated, so that their capacity to move glucose is exceeded (transport maximum, or T m ). In males, the maximum amount of glucose that can be recovered is about 375 mg/min, whereas in females, it is about 300 mg/min. This recovery rate translates to an arterial concentration of about 200 mg/dL. Though an exceptionally high sugar intake might cause sugar to appear briefly in the urine, the appearance of glycosuria usually points to type I or II diabetes mellitus. The transport of glucose from the lumen of the PCT to the interstitial space is similar to the way it is absorbed by the small intestine.

===

[11] (p.1142) The Urinary System > Physiology of Urine Formation > Glomerular Filtration Rate (GFR)
---
Context from previous chunk: Osmosis is the movement of solvent (water) across a membrane that is impermeable to a solute in the solution. This creates a pressure, osmotic pressure, which will exist until the solute concentration is the same on both sides of a semipermeable membrane. As long as the concentration differs, water will move. Glomerular filtration occurs when glomerular hydrostatic pressure exceeds the luminal hydrostatic pressure of Bowman’s capsule.

===

[12] (p.1142) The Urinary System > Physiology of Urine Formation > Glomerular Filtration Rate (GFR)
---
There is an opposing force, the osmotic pressure, which is typically higher in the glomerular capillary. Osmotic pressure is exerted by the solutes inside the lumen of the capillary as well as inside of Bowman's capsule. Since the filtration membrane limits the size of particles crossing the membrane, the osmotic pressure inside the glomerular capillary is higher than the osmotic pressure in Bowman's capsule. Cells and the medium-to-large proteins cannot pass between the podocyte processes or through the fenestrations of the capillary endothelial cells. Red and white blood cells, platelets, albumins, and other proteins too large to pass through the filter remain in the capillary, creating an average colloid osmotic pressure of 30 mm Hg within the capillary. The absence of proteins in Bowman's space results in an osmotic pressure near zero. The only pressure moving fluid across the capillary wall into the lumen of Bowman's space is hydrostatic pressure. Hydrostatic pressure is sufficient to push water through the membrane despite the osmotic pressure working against it. The sum of all of the influences, both osmotic and hydrostatic, results in a net filtration pressure (NFP) of about 10 mm Hg. A proper concentration of solutes in the blood is important in maintaining osmotic pressure both in the glomerulus and systemically.

===

The absence of proteins in Bowman's space results in an osmotic pressure near zero. The sum of all of the influences, both osmotic and hydrostatic, results in a net filtration pressure (NFP) of about 10 mm Hg. A proper concentration of solutes in the blood is important in maintaining osmotic pressure both in the glomerulus and systemically.

[14] (p.1142) The Urinary System > Physiology of Urine Formation > Glomerular Filtration Rate (GFR)
---
There are disorders in which too much protein passes through the filtration slits into the kidney filtrate. This excess protein in the filtrate leads to a deficiency of circulating plasma proteins. In turn, the presence of protein in the urine increases its osmolarity; this holds more water in the filtrate and results in an increase in urine volume. Because there is less circulating protein, principally albumin, the osmotic pressure of the blood falls.

---
The kidneys are innervated by the sympathetic neurons of the autonomic nervous system via the celiac plexus and splanchnic nerves. Reduction of sympathetic stimulation results in vasodilation and increased blood flow through the kidneys during resting conditions.

[17] (p.1153) The Urinary System > Regulation of Renal Blood Flow > Sympathetic Nerves
---
Context from previous chunk: Intercalated cells reabsorb K + and HCO 3 – while secreting H + . This function lowers the acidity of the plasma while increasing the acidity of the urine. The kidneys are innervated by the sympathetic neurons of the autonomic nervous system via the celiac plexus and splanchnic nerves. Reduction of sympathetic stimulation results in vasodilation and increased blood flow through the kidneys during resting conditions.

[18] (p.1153, score=1.00) The Urinary System > Regulation of Renal Blood Flow > Sympathetic Nerves
---
When the frequency of action potentials increases, the arteriolar smooth muscle constricts (vasoconstriction), resulting in diminished glomerular flow, so less filtration occurs. Under conditions of stress, sympathetic nervous activity increases, resulting in the direct vasoconstriction of afferent arterioles (norepinephrine effect) as well as stimulation of the adrenal medulla. The adrenal medulla, in turn, produces a generalized vasoconstriction through the release of epinephrine. This includes vasoconstriction of the afferent arterioles, further reducing the volume of blood flowing through the kidneys. This process redirects blood to other organs with more immediate needs. If blood pressure falls, the sympathetic nerves will also stimulate the release of renin. Additional renin increases production of the powerful vasoconstrictor angiotensin II. Angiotensin II, as discussed above, will also stimulate aldosterone production to augment blood volume through retention of more Na + and water. Only a 10 mm Hg pressure differential across the glomerulus is required for normal GFR, so very small changes in afferent arterial pressure significantly increase or decrease GFR. Autoregulation The kidneys are very effective at regulating the rate of blood flow over a wide range of blood pressures.

===

[19] (p.1153) The Urinary System > Regulation of Renal Blood Flow > Sympathetic Nerves
---
The kidneys are innervated by the sympathetic neurons of the autonomic nervous system via the celiac plexus and splanchnic nerves. Reduction of sympathetic stimulation results in vasodilation and increased blood flow through the kidneys during resting conditions. When the frequency of action potentials increases, the arteriolar smooth muscle constricts (vasoconstriction), resulting in diminished glomerular flow, so less filtration occurs. Under conditions of stress, sympathetic nervous activity increases, resulting in the direct vasoconstriction of afferent arterioles (norepinephrine effect) as well as stimulation of the adrenal medulla. The adrenal medulla, in turn, produces a generalized vasoconstriction through the release of epinephrine. This includes vasoconstriction of the afferent arterioles, further reducing the volume of blood flowing through the kidneys. This process redirects blood to other organs with more immediate needs. If blood pressure falls, the sympathetic nerves will also stimulate the release of renin. Additional renin increases production of the powerful vasoconstrictor angiotensin II. Angiotensin II will also stimulate aldosterone production to augment blood volume through retention of more Na+ and water. Only a 10 mm Hg pressure differential across the glomerulus is required for normal GFR, so very small changes in afferent arterial pressure significantly increase or decrease GFR. Autoregulation The kidneys are very effective at regulating the rate of blood flow over a wide range of blood pressures.

===

[20] (p.1153) The Urinary System > Regulation of Renal Blood Flow > Sympathetic Nerves
---
Despite changes in blood pressure, the filtration rate through the kidney will change very little. This is due to two internal autoregulatory mechanisms that operate without outside influence: the myogenic mechanism and the tubuloglomerular feedback mechanism. The myogenic mechanism regulating blood flow within the kidney depends upon a characteristic shared by most smooth muscle cells of the body. When you stretch a smooth muscle cell, it contracts; when you stop, it relaxes, restoring its resting length. This mechanism works in the afferent arteriole that supplies the glomerulus. When blood pressure increases, smooth muscle cells in the wall of the arteriole are stretched and respond by contracting to resist the pressure, resulting in little change in flow. When blood pressure drops, the same smooth muscle cells relax to lower resistance, allowing a continued even flow of blood. The tubuloglomerular feedback mechanism involves the JGA and a paracrine signaling mechanism utilizing ATP, adenosine, and nitric oxide (NO). This mechanism stimulates either contraction or relaxation of afferent arteriolar smooth muscle cells.

===

[21] (p.1142) The Urinary System > Physiology of Urine Formation > Glomerular Filtration Rate (GFR)
---
The volume of filtrate formed by both kidneys per minute is termed the glomerular filtration rate (GFR). The heart pumps about 5 L blood per min under resting conditions.

===

[22] (p.1142) The Urinary System > Physiology of Urine Formation > Glomerular Filtration Rate (GFR)
---
A number of symptoms, signs, or laboratory findings point to the diagnosis of kidney failure. Symptoms of kidney failure include weakness, lethargy, shortness of breath, widespread edema, anemia, metabolic acidosis, metabolic alkalosis, heart arrhythmias, uremia (high urea level in the blood), loss of appetite, fatigue, excessive urination, and oliguria (too little urine output). The volume of filtrate formed by both kidneys per minute is termed the glomerular filtration rate (GFR). The heart pumps about 5 L blood per min under resting conditions.

===

[23] (p.1142, score=1.00) The Urinary System > Physiology of Urine Formation > Glomerular Filtration Rate (GFR)
---
Approximately 20 percent or one liter enters the kidneys to be filtered. On average, this liter results in the production of about 125 mL/min filtrate produced in males (range of 90 to 140 mL/min) and 105 mL/min filtrate produced in females (range of 80 to 125 mL/min). This amount equates to a volume of about 180 L/day in males and 150 L/day in females. Ninety-nine percent of this filtrate is returned to the circulation by reabsorption so that only about 1–2 liters of urine are produced per day. Calculating Urine Formation per Day: Renal blood flow is 1050 mL/min. Cardiac output is about 5000 mL/minute, of which 21 percent flows through the kidney. Renal plasma flow is 578 mL/min. Renal plasma flow equals the blood flow per minute times the hematocrit. If a person has a hematocrit of 45, then the renal plasma flow is 55 percent. Glomerular filtration rate is 110 mL/min. The GFR is the amount of plasma entering Bowman's capsule per minute. It is the renal plasma flow times the fraction that enters the renal capsule (19 percent). Urine production is 1296 mL/day. The filtrate not recovered by the kidney is the urine that will be eliminated. It is the GFR times the fraction of the filtrate that is not reabsorbed (0.8 percent). GFR is influenced by the hydrostatic pressure and colloid osmotic pressure on either side of the capillary membrane of the glomerulus.

===

[24] (p.1142) The Urinary System > Physiology of Urine Formation > Glomerular Filtration Rate (GFR)
---
The volume of filtrate formed by both kidneys per minute is termed the glomerular filtration rate (GFR). The heart pumps about 5 L blood per min under resting conditions. Approximately 20 percent or one liter enters the kidneys to be filtered. On average, this liter results in the production of about 125 mL/min filtrate produced in males (range of 90 to 140 mL/min) and 105 mL/min filtrate produced in females (range of 80 to 125 mL/min). This amount equates to a volume of about 180 L/day in males and 150 L/day in females. Ninety-nine percent of this filtrate is returned to the circulation by reabsorption so that only about 1–2 liters of urine are produced per day. Renal blood flow is about 1050 mL/min, which represents 21 percent of cardiac output of about 5000 mL/minute. Renal plasma flow equals the blood flow per minute times the hematocrit; if a person has a hematocrit of 45, then the renal plasma flow is 55 percent, equaling 578 mL plasma/min. The GFR is the amount of plasma entering Bowman's capsule per minute. It is the renal plasma flow times the fraction that enters the renal capsule (19 percent), equaling 110 mL filtrate/min. The filtrate not recovered by the kidney is the urine that will be eliminated. It is the GFR times the fraction of the filtrate that is not reabsorbed (0.8 percent), equaling 0.9 mL urine/min, which multiplied by 60 minutes times 24 hours yields daily urine production of 1296 mL/day.

===

[25] (p.1142) The Urinary System > Physiology of Urine Formation > Glomerular Filtration Rate (GFR)
---
Filtration occurs as pressure forces fluid and solutes through a semipermeable barrier with the solute movement constrained by particle size.

Tutor: I hear you—let's pause the reading gap for a moment and just use your intuition. If I pump water out of a cup into a bucket next to it repeatedly, does the bucket get more full or less full?
Student: Um, I'm not really sure honestly—I don't think we covered this in the reading or anything.
Tutor: I notice we've circled on the same analogy—let me be more direct now. Sodium is actively pumped out of the tubule into the tissue, creating a concentration gradient. Water then moves passively across the membrane to dilute that high sodium concentration—this is called osmosis. Can you explain why water would move toward the higher sodium concentration?

You are the Dean handling the topic-selection phase of a tutoring session in human anatomy.
The student has not yet locked to a specific topic. Your job is to read their latest
message, classify what they want, and write the tutor's next utterance in your own
voice (no templates, no canned strings).

Classify the student's latest message as exactly one intent:
  topic_query       — names or describes a human anatomy topic they want to learn.
                      INCLUDES: questions, statements/assertions, leading claims,
                      hedged or anxious phrasings, and one-word noun phrases — as
                      long as a human anatomy topic is present in the message.
  card_pick         — references one of the card options (number, label, close paraphrase)
  greeting          — "hi", "hello", starting the session
  distress          — emotional venting or frustration ("ugh", "i'm stressed", "this is hard", expletive)
  off_topic         — unrelated to human anatomy (small talk, jokes, non-sequiturs)
  ambiguous         — too short or too generic to route (single pronoun, empty, filler)

For topic_query and card_pick, extract `normalized_topic` — the cleanest topic name
suitable for retrieval (strip hedges, numbering, affirmations). For card_pick, set it
to the referenced option's text (not a number). For other intents, leave it empty.

CRITICAL: students phrase topic queries in many shapes. Treat ALL of these as
topic_query and extract the topic noun phrase even when the student is hedging,
asserting, or just naming a single noun:

  Student: "What is the deltoid muscle?"
  → intent=topic_query, normalized_topic="deltoid muscle"

  Student: "Reflexes are basically just instant nerve reactions, right?"
  → intent=topic_query, normalized_topic="reflexes"  (statement IS a topic_query;
    the leading assertion doesn't disqualify it. Still topic = reflexes.)

  Student: "I'm not sure I really understand chemical digestion — could we go through it?"
  → intent=topic_query, normalized_topic="chemical digestion"  (hedging doesn't
    disqualify; topic is clearly named.)

  Student: "elbow joint"
  → intent=topic_query, normalized_topic="elbow joint"  (one-word/short-phrase
    topics are valid; do NOT classify as ambiguous if a domain noun is present.)

  Student: "umm so... like, what's that thing in the kidney that filters blood?"
  → intent=topic_query, normalized_topic="kidney filtration"  (vague but
    clearly anatomy; extract the best topic phrase.)

  Student: "ugh i'm so confused"
  → intent=distress, normalized_topic=""

  Student: "what should I make for dinner"
  → intent=off_topic, normalized_topic=""

Rule of thumb: if you can identify ANY human anatomy noun phrase in the message
that the student seems interested in learning about, classify as topic_query and
extract that phrase as normalized_topic. Reserve ambiguous ONLY for truly empty
or pronoun-only messages.

For non-topic intents (greeting, distress, off_topic, ambiguous) write `tutor_reply` —
a short, warm, contextual reply that acknowledges what the student actually said and
invites them to name a specific topic. Match the student's emotional register. Do NOT
repeat their words back inside quotes. Do NOT use a template phrase. 1-3 sentences.
If cards are currently on screen, gently remind the student they can pick one. When
the intent is topic_query or card_pick, leave `tutor_reply` as an empty string — the
routing layer will handle it.

Never ask multiple questions. Never reveal answers. Never produce headers or lists.

  "tutor_reply": "",
  "rationale": ""
}}

[1] (p.1148) The Urinary System > Tubular Reabsorption > Reabsorption and Secretion in the PCT
---
The first point at which the forming urine is modified is in the PCT. Here, some substances are reabsorbed, whereas others are secreted. All of these substances were absorbed in the digestive tract—99 percent of the water and most of the solutes filtered by the nephron must be reabsorbed. Water and substances that are reabsorbed are returned to the circulation by the peritubular and vasa recta capillaries. It is important to understand the difference between the glomerulus and the peritubular and vasa recta capillaries. The glomerulus has a relatively high pressure inside its capillaries and can sustain this by dilating the afferent arteriole while constricting the efferent arteriole. This assures adequate filtration pressure even as the systemic blood pressure varies.

===

[2] (p.1148) The Urinary System > Tubular Reabsorption > Reabsorption and Secretion in the PCT
---
Movement of water into the peritubular capillaries and vasa recta will be influenced primarily by osmolarity and concentration gradients. Sodium is actively pumped out of the PCT into the interstitial spaces between cells and diffuses down its concentration gradient into the peritubular capillary. As it does so, water will follow passively to maintain an isotonic fluid environment inside the capillary. This is called obligatory water reabsorption, because water is "obliged" to follow the Na+. More substances move across the membranes of the PCT than any other portion of the nephron.

===

[3] (p.1148, score=1.00) The Urinary System > Tubular Reabsorption > Reabsorption and Secretion in the PCT
---
The glomerulus has a relatively high pressure inside its capillaries and can sustain this by dilating the afferent arteriole while constricting the efferent arteriole. This assures adequate filtration pressure even as the systemic blood pressure varies. Movement of water into the peritubular capillaries and vasa recta will be influenced primarily by osmolarity and concentration gradients. Sodium is actively pumped out of the PCT into the interstitial spaces between cells and diffuses down its concentration gradient into the peritubular capillary. As it does so, water will follow passively to maintain an isotonic fluid environment inside the capillary. This is called obligatory water reabsorption, because water is "obliged" to follow the Na+. More substances move across the membranes of the PCT than any other portion of the nephron.

===

[4] (p.1148) The Urinary System > Tubular Reabsorption > Reabsorption and Secretion in the PCT
---
Many of these substances (amino acids and glucose) use symport mechanisms for transport along with Na+.

===

[5] (p.1148) The Urinary System > Tubular Reabsorption > Reabsorption and Secretion in the PCT
---
This is called obligatory water reabsorption, because water is "obliged" to follow the Na+. More substances move across the membranes of the PCT than any other portion of the nephron. Many of these substances (amino acids and glucose) use symport mechanisms for transport along with Na+.

===

[6] (p.1148) The Urinary System > Tubular Reabsorption > Reabsorption and Secretion in the PCT
---
Antiport, active transport, diffusion, and facilitated diffusion are additional mechanisms by which substances are moved from one side of a membrane to the other. Recall that cells have two surfaces: apical and basal.

===

[7] (p.1148) The Urinary System > Tubular Reabsorption > Reabsorption and Secretion in the PCT
---
Context from previous chunk: Many of these substances (amino acids and glucose) use symport mechanisms for transport along with Na + . Antiport, active transport, diffusion, and facilitated diffusion are additional mechanisms by which substances are moved from one side of a membrane to the other. Recall that cells have two surfaces: apical and basal.

===

[8] (p.1148, score=1.00) The Urinary System > Tubular Reabsorption > Reabsorption and Secretion in the PCT
---
The apical surface is the one facing the lumen or open space of a cavity or tube, in this case, the inside of the PCT. The basal surface of the cell faces the connective tissue base to which the cell attaches (basement membrane) or the cell membrane closer to the basement membrane if there is a stratified layer of cells. In the PCT, there is a single layer of simple cuboidal endothelial cells against the basement membrane.

===

[9] (p.1148) The Urinary System > Tubular Reabsorption > Reabsorption and Secretion in the PCT
---
Context from previous chunk: Antiport, active transport, diffusion, and facilitated diffusion are additional mechanisms by which substances are moved from one side of a membrane to the other. Recall that cells have two surfaces: apical and basal. The apical surface is the one facing the lumen or open space of a cavity or tube, in this case, the inside of the PCT. The basal surface of the cell faces the connective tissue base to which the cell attaches (basement membrane) or the cell membrane closer to the basement membrane if there is a stratified layer of cells. In the PCT, there is a single layer of simple cuboidal endothelial cells against the basement membrane.

===

[10] (p.1148) The Urinary System > Tubular Reabsorption > Reabsorption and Secretion in the PCT
---
The numbers and particular types of pumps and channels vary between the apical and basilar surfaces. A few of the substances that are transported with Na + (symport mechanism) on the apical membrane include Cl – , Ca ++ , amino acids, and glucose. Sodium is actively exchanged for K + using ATP on the basal membrane. Most of the substances transported by a symport mechanism on the apical membrane are transported by facilitated diffusion on the basal membrane. At least three ions, K + , Ca ++ , and Mg ++ , diffuse laterally between adjacent cell membranes (transcellular). About 67 percent of the water, Na + , and K + entering the nephron is reabsorbed in the PCT and returned to the circulation. Almost 100 percent of glucose, amino acids, and other organic substances such as vitamins are normally recovered here. Some glucose may appear in the urine if circulating glucose levels are high enough that all the glucose transporters in the PCT are saturated, so that their capacity to move glucose is exceeded (transport maximum, or T m ). In males, the maximum amount of glucose that can be recovered is about 375 mg/min, whereas in females, it is about 300 mg/min. This recovery rate translates to an arterial concentration of about 200 mg/dL. Though an exceptionally high sugar intake might cause sugar to appear briefly in the urine, the appearance of glycosuria usually points to type I or II diabetes mellitus. The transport of glucose from the lumen of the PCT to the interstitial space is similar to the way it is absorbed by the small intestine.
